speaker: interviewer
text: --with Wanda Mehr. We were talking about-- last tape, about you going to prison. What was the name of this prison, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prison had the name of the street. It was Laskiego. And was very well-known prison, mostly for political prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a lot, then-- mostly the wives of the Russian officers who left with the army and they left behind their wives. They couldn't take-- because they were in a hurry to get out, because the Germans were from the West Poland, coming forward. So they're in a hurry. They fled and left the wife behind. And the Germans wanted to find out about their wives. They interrogated them about the Russian officers. But they didn't tell them anything. And they said that, in their gardens and their homes, they hid some war [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] documents or whatever they searched for. But they didn't know about it. They couldn't tell them anything. But they imprisoned them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this already in 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle of winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- no, that was end of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: December '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we backtrack a little bit? At this time, when you were sent to that prison, what did you know about the rest of your family? Where was your younger sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know anything. They took me from the street-- I told you before. And I had no idea what will happen in the prison or what happened to my family. Because, as I said before, my parents were in Grodek, not in Lwow-- and my youngest brother. One of the sisters was at the hospital in Lwow. And they killed all the inmates of the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: This already happened before you went to prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I found out later. I didn't know. I was not in contact with anybody with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that your younger sister went to Lublin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she, from Lublin, was deported, I don't know exactly, from the street. I think from the street she was deported to Germany for forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before you went to prison, that she was deported--

speaker: interviewee
text: That must happened before, but I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: She went to Lublin out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: She told me later. I didn't know. She left the ghetto, yes. And the Polish woman who gave her the birth certificate told her to go to Lublin. I don't know, by train or how she went there, I don't know it exactly. And from there-- but that she didn't know about me, about what happened to my child. She didn't know anything. We found out about each other when we found each other again after the-- years after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were sent to the prison, at that time your older sister was still in the ghetto with her husband and family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, until-- yes, until they liquidated the ghetto. But she was before. She went out-- I told you that her husband worked for the Italians. There was the Italian army at the same place where the Germans were, standing. And that was in a park. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't-- I told you once, I think. That's right. So did she leave the ghetto before you went to prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in contact with them. I don't know, because I went to the-- because when my child was in hospital, I went to the Polish woman, and that happened then. But I had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your older sister's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: The eldest sister? She came from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. She had a daughter. You said she had a young--

speaker: interviewee
text: My eldest sister had a young child-- a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: A boy, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Little boy. I think he was must have been around five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, the boy? [? Anatoly. ?] And they called him [INAUDIBLE]. [? Anatoly-- ?] [? Anatole. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that your older brother went to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he go to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the very beginning, when the-- when the Germans advanced, when the Russians flew. When the Russians left Lwow, everybody knows that the Germans are advancing, and everybody was scared. They didn't know exactly what will happen, but people were afraid. There were some-- some people coming, those that we called them 'bezhentsy'-- not we, the Russians called them 'bezhentsy'-- from Warsaw, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where the-- yes, where Germans were already. And they told stories what happened there. And they fled to our side, to the eastern side of Lwow. And so then and it happened to all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that he had a child. Was that a boy, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, the older-- my brother-- he was, for a long time, my only brother. [NON-ENGLISH], they call older brother. And he fled-- talked it over with his wife. And they had a child of a few weeks, only-- the first child. Young married-- both early married. And he fled. All men-- most young men fled on foot. They ran all to Russia. But it was a long way to run to Russia, on foot. And the Germans already, from the air, shot them all-- most-- I don't know how many were on the roads, there. But most people fled to Russia, especially men. And his wife, with a pram and a little baby, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how she was thinking-- nobody was normal, then. Otherwise she knew, it's too a long a way to-- to run on foot with a baby in a pram to her parents, who lived nearer to Russia-- nearly on the border, in a small township on the border. Though she wanted to run to her parents, there. But, as I never heard of anything, she was killed on the road, running. Because from the air they shoot them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a boy, or a girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the child I don't remember. But I remember her-- my brother's wife. She was Rosa Kimmel. And the baby, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, at the time when you went to prison, was your younger sister still alive in the hospital? Did you have any news about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was an older, not younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your younger sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the younger one was the one that was liberated, that survived-- is survivor. And the other one was an older one. And no, I don't know what happened to her. She was at the hospital. And she was very ill. She had-- was at a hospital in Lwow. And later I heard that they shot them-- they killed them all at the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a last question about--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't seen her-- my mother haven't seen her before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when you started prison in Lwow, your parents were still in Grodek.

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you started in prison, in Lwow, your parents were still alive in Grodek, with your youngest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't think so. I don't think so. That was in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison. And I think they were killed, first, there. I told you, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --city, there. I think they were killed before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they already were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because my mother still came, at the beginning, and told us the story about the little brother. And then she went back. And I think-- no, they killed them first. It was earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you got the story about the prison, can you give us a brief picture of-- of your life in the ghetto, for the two weeks? What did it look like? What sort of things happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't think it was very interesting. There was not to much to say about it, because people were huddled together, in those little rooms. And no one could get out or in. And the-- the Judenrat, the committee of those Jews, had always from the Germans-- from them they got directions what to do-- to deliver them so many, so-and-so many Jews for work. And they had to leave-- to deliver to them. And if they didn't deliver-- They went around from house to house, and no one could escape it. They didn't know-- for work, or to kill them. They didn't know. But they have to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went from house to house. And if they liked it or not, they had to have the people in such-and-such number of them. And that's what they had to deliver to the Germans. Do you remember any-- the names of any people from the Judenrat in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew them. I never knew-- first of all, I was much younger, because they were older people. And they were men who I don't know. There was a rabbi. I never knew him. And they were-- and some former wealthy merchants. They knew whom to pick. They picked out those people, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they asked him to do that, whatever they had to do. They didn't come by themselves, even for killings. This Judenrat had to deliver the people. And, as a matter of fact, the Jews there were angry, because if someone hid somewhere-- but they blamed the Judenrat for it. But I think the Judenrat had to do it because they were pressured by the Germans. And if they wouldn't do it, they would kill them, too. So they had to-- to deliver so-and-so many men. Some didn't come back. Some they killed. Others, only to work there. What they did, I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any form of rationing? Did they actually give people rations of food, from the Judenrat or from any other wealthy organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give anything. They said they don't have it, themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- any soup kitchens at all, in the ghetto? Were there any soup kitchens?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think who-- who had money could buy some little piece of bread, that was all. But they didn't give anything to people, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any types of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It could be in other ghettos, in other place, it was different. But that what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any types of shops open?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. No. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of food did you survive on when you were in the ghetto for two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember any-- any bread that we had. I don't remember. People didn't cook there. They didn't have any food. I don't know how they survived and how I survived. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The place you stayed in there had running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be that it was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because I remember, at nighttime, when I left with my little daughter, there was light, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about heating? Any form of heating possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No one had heating, no. Because they had to buy it. They couldn't go out. They couldn't buy. We didn't have any stores there, any shops there. No, there was no heating. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: If people did any food preparation, was it on-- what sort of stove-- was it on wood, or on gas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any stoves there, electric or gas, and I don't remember cooking there. No one cooked there. I was only two weeks. But what-- as [INAUDIBLE] later, I don't remember any cooking, anything, no. There was no food. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about facilities for washing? Were there any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No one washed, there, no one. People never changed their clothing. No one thought of any kind of hygiene there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the two weeks you were in the ghetto, did you see or come to know about any particular diseases? Were there any major diseases, at that time, in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diseases, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the major ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diphtheria and common-- what you call now flu. That was not a flu. It was different. Colds. I don't know. I don't remember people seeing a doctor, even. If one survived, so you survived. If you died-- I don't remember what they did with corpses. I don't-- I haven't seen any, in the two weeks there. Must-- haven't seen if they buried them anywhere, or going out of the ghetto. I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any corpses on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't seen any. I remember only once-- I-- maybe they had some. I don't know. I remember I had no food at all. I don't know how we survived without food. But no one gave us anything. But I remember that, when I was there, they didn't take to work women-- young women with children. And they came in, looking around-- it was always panic. They looked around, if someone is on the-- someone went out of the house, they grabbed him straightaway, or they hit him. I remember once that-- I never mentioned it. But the Germans paid for it. Because you can still see it on my head, there, so many years, and there it still is. You can find out. I went to a doctor here. And not a known doctor, but to a German doctor I had to go. Otherwise they wouldn't have paid me for it. I went out, left my child. I don't know what it was that I went out or something. And whoever was on the streets there, outside the house, they beat them up-- they did anything, you know-- hit him, or threw him. There were big stones near the gate. It was somewhere near the gate. I don't remember why I went out, looking for some-- for somebody, someone. And I went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were big stones. And a German asked me, where do you go? He thought I wanted-- it was near the gate that I want to go out. And I did that I don't understand, even. I didn't answer [INAUDIBLE]. So he hit me, threw me down because I didn't answer him. It wasn't Gestapo. It was must be an SS man. They came in. You could find them always in the ghetto, around, looking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hit my head against the stone. It was bleeding. And there was-- I couldn't go to a doctor visit. It wasn't broken, the bone, but a cut-- a deep cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in. He didn't say anything. He left me. I fell. I got up and ran back quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was this doctor that later helped me with the child. They gave me the injection. And I couldn't go out, and I couldn't go to see a doctor, and there were no doctors. Maybe there was one in another building, but I didn't know. But this doctor, that [INAUDIBLE], he had something visit. And he just sew it together without anaesthetic, without anything. He sewed the skin together. And it healed up, without anything. Not only anesthetic he did it, I didn't have anything, even not a bit of alcohol, nothing. And it just healed up. And it still-- you can still see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any typhus, in the ghetto? Did you see any typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only two weeks. And I didn't know the people. There were people from all over Lwow. I didn't know them. I knew only my immediate family. And in two weeks' time I was always with my child, so-- And other people, yes, they came in. And the Judenrat had to deliver so much, such a quantity, you know, of people to work. They went out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, after the two weeks, I went out with the child, nighttime. When I came back, they were still there. And then I lost contact. I don't know. I know how my sister went out. I told you, with their little boy, that he worked with the Italians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I know. And I know, later, what happened to them. Because they didn't go back to the ghetto. They stayed with the German divisions there. I it was in-- in a park-- a big, big, park. But I forgot the name of the park.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told you how-- my sister died with her little boy. She gave herself up, on the street, when they took her. When the Italians broke up with the Germans, they went back. So the Germans took over. He was a translator. [INAUDIBLE], or how you call it, here-- with the Italians. They took him, and they killed him. And my sister was left there. She couldn't be there anymore with her child hidden, because the Italians left. And she had nowhere to go. And because they killed her husband, she went out with the child to the first Gestapo man on the street and gave herself up, with the child. What happened to her, I had no idea. They killed her and the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her son was called [? Anatole. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Anatole, ?] yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: The married name was Erb.

speaker: interviewer
text: And maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spiegler. I was Spiegler, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see-- did people have much lice, in the ghetto, the people that you saw around you? Was there much lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto? There were only white-- white thin faces, hundreds of them around everywhere, and huddled one near the other one. And no one went out. And everybody wanted to know what will happen tomorrow. And that was how it was going on. Until the ghetto was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what sort of work people were sent out to do? What sort of work they had to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they took them out? Yes. What they did there, I don't know. Cleaned, I think-- cleaned them and scrubbed and whatever they did. In the German-- how do you call it-- was a [GERMAN] or whatever, there. They stood there. There were a few thousand. Park Stryiskyi. I gave it to you. You have it written down. It came back to me. It comes back if I-- When I talk, I don't think of it. But when I think deeper, it comes back. Stryiskyi Park, it was. They called it park. It was more a huge one-- a big one. But what they did there, I think-- women sewing their military clothing-- sewing, cleaning, that was-- or maybe for their kitchen, they cook-- they peeled potatoes, or they prepared for-- they had a big kitchen for the army, there. And that's what they did there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get out of the ghetto, the second time? Before you went to prison, how did you get out of the ghetto, the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I? I didn't go-- I was not in the ghetto. I came back from the hospital, there, because I had nowhere to go. I forgot about this in my pocket, that I had the paper of that-- address of that Polish woman who helped my sister-- younger sister. But from the hospital I went back. And I think that I stayed only one night there. And next day, when I discovered the paper that I had-- that woman-- and because I didn't have the child-- she didn't want to take me with the child. So I went to her. And I told you before how I came into her place-- to the Polish woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: You visited your sister in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went out from the ghetto, the eldest sister, with her husband, when they went to work in the morning. That was before the ghetto was liquidated. But I wasn't there when it was liquidated. Not long later, after this, it was liquidated. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told you how they took out the child. Because going to work they would take away from him the child. He tied him up to his waist, under a big coat, and covered him with the coat. And that's how they came in there. And as they came in there, he was straightaway assigned to the Italians. And my sister was hidden there by the Italians. They were very good to them. But I haven't seen since them since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came--

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back from the hospital the first time, did you go back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You went back to the woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I already had-- was with the woman staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to her and told her how the child is there. And about two weeks or over two weeks, when I came the last time and have seen my child-- I told you how she looked. And the sister there said, you can take her home. So I said-- and about a day or two days later, because I can't go all the time with her, and I couldn't carry her. She was too heavy to carry her back. And I was afraid of the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came back and told the Polish woman. And somehow I convinced her. And she went to take to the hospital to take. And she brought her home. But she could go by tram, as a Christian, of course. And she brought her back to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't-- as I said, I couldn't even say goodbye to my child, because she hurried me. She said, look, now, as you're here with your child, someone, after the prison-- it was after the prison. She said, the police is around everywhere. They can find out. Your child I can keep, but I can't keep you any longer. So you have to go out on the street and let yourself, somehow. There were always-- they always rounded up people to send for forced labor to Germany. And that's how they caught me on the street. And you know the story how it-- with that Ukrainian, when I came into that camp, waiting for a train, and how he saved me. And since then I haven't seen my daughter. I've been in Germany until the American army liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Final question about the ghetto. Who was guarding the ghetto? Who was on the exits? What sort of people? Were they Ukrainians, Germans, or Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only the Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No one. The Germans came in-- Gestapo-- came in, every day, in the morning, counting the people. The Judenrat knew exactly how many people are in the ghetto. And they had to give him-- them report, you know, reporting to them how many people are there. Unless someone died, I don't know. I haven't seen it. For two weeks, I haven't seen much in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's just Jewish policemen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, only. Because they left everything to them. And if they didn't do it correctly, what they wanted, they just killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the beginning of prison for you. Can you please continue the story about the truck taking you to the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came? Yes, the truck came over, and they let us out and opened the-- the prison was a very tall building, I remember-- a huge building, dark grey building. And there was a huge entrance door, a heavy one they opened and let us in. And a group had to go up-- I think it was the second floor. I had to go up there. And they counted how many people go in here, how many people there. There were a few rooms. I don't know about the others. I know the one that they let me in. And a few people with me came in. It was a little-- a little room, half of this one. And inside were a few hundred people, women only. And they were all standing. There was no room to sit down or lay down. We were all standing. One supported the other one, back to back. That's how we stood there, for half a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue next time.
